definition:
Singular subject → singular verb. Plural subject → plural verb.
Trick: ignore phrases between subject and verb.
e.g. 'The box of chocolates IS on the table.' (box = subject, not chocolates)

In 'there is/are' sentences, the subject FOLLOWS the verb.
e.g. 'There ARE three issues.' (issues = subject, plural → are)
Don't let 'there' fool you into using a singular verb.

The verb agrees with the NEARER subject.
e.g. 'Neither the students nor the teacher IS ready.'
e.g. 'Neither the teacher nor the students ARE ready.'
Remember: proximity rule.

These are always SINGULAR:
each, every, either, neither, one, anyone, everyone, someone, no one, anybody, everybody, somebody, nobody
e.g. 'Each of the students IS responsible.'

Words like team, group, committee, board, jury, company = SINGULAR on GMAT.
e.g. 'The committee HAS reached a decision.'
Don't be misled by the implied plurality of the noun.

A pronoun must agree in NUMBER and GENDER with its antecedent.
Singular antecedent → singular pronoun
e.g. 'Each student must submit HIS OR HER work.' (not 'their')
GMAT often tests this with collective nouns and indefinite pronouns.

A pronoun must refer clearly to ONE specific antecedent.
Ambiguous: 'When John met Bob, he was nervous.' (who was nervous?)
Fix: Replace the ambiguous pronoun with the actual noun.

WHO = subject (does the action): 'Who called?'
WHOM = object (receives the action): 'To whom did you speak?'
Test: replace with he/him. If 'he' sounds right → who. If 'him' → whom.

Subject pronouns: I, he, she, we, they, who
Object pronouns: me, him, her, us, them, whom
e.g. 'Between you and ME' (not 'I')
e.g. 'The award went to HIM and ME' (not 'he and I')

Items in a list or comparison must use the SAME grammatical form.
Wrong: 'She likes hiking, to swim, and dancing.'
Right: 'She likes hiking, swimming, and dancing.'
Match verb forms, noun forms, or clause types consistently.

These pairs require parallel structure:
• not only X but also Y
• either X or Y
• neither X nor Y
• both X and Y
e.g. 'Not only did she WIN but also she CELEBRATED.' ✓

You must compare the same types of things.
Wrong: 'The economy of China is larger than France.'
Right: 'The economy of China is larger than THAT OF France.'
Use 'that of' or 'those of' to make comparisons grammatically parallel.

A modifier must be placed IMMEDIATELY next to the word it modifies.
Wrong: 'Running quickly, the finish line was crossed.'
Right: 'Running quickly, SHE crossed the finish line.'
The subject after a comma must be the one doing the action in the opening phrase.

A modifier is 'dangling' when the noun it should modify is ABSENT from the sentence.
Wrong: 'Having studied all night, the exam was easy.' (exam didn't study)
Right: 'Having studied all night, SHE found the exam easy.'

A modifier appears next to the WRONG noun.
Wrong: 'She almost drove her kids to school every day.' (implies she rarely drove)
Right: 'She drove her kids to school almost every day.'
Move the modifier to be adjacent to what it modifies.

Simple past: completed action at specific time → 'She submitted the report.'
Present perfect: action relevant to now → 'She HAS submitted the report.'
Past perfect: action completed BEFORE another past event → 'She HAD submitted before the meeting.'

Use subjunctive for hypotheticals and recommendations:
• Hypothetical: 'If I WERE you…' (not 'was')
• Demand/suggest/require: 'The board required that he BE present.' (not 'is')
Signal words: if, wish, as if, recommend, require, demand, suggest, insist

THAT → essential clause (no commas): identifies which one
'The report that was filed yesterday is lost.'
WHICH → non-essential clause (with commas): adds extra info
'The report, which was filed yesterday, is lost.'
Remove the which-clause — the sentence should still make sense.

Countable (can be pluralized):
→ fewer, many, number of
e.g. fewer mistakes, many students

Uncountable (mass nouns):
→ less, much, amount of
e.g. less water, much effort

LIKE compares NOUNS (no verb following):
'Like her mother, she excels at math.'
AS introduces a CLAUSE (with a verb):
'Do AS I say, not AS I do.'
Wrong: 'Like I said…' → Right: 'As I said…'

DUE TO = adjective phrase, modifies a NOUN:
'The cancellation WAS due to rain.' (modifies 'cancellation')
BECAUSE OF = adverb phrase, modifies a VERB:
'The game was cancelled BECAUSE OF rain.' (modifies 'was cancelled')
Test: replace with 'caused by' — if it fits, use 'due to'.

BETWEEN: for exactly TWO things
'The deal was between the two companies.'
AMONG: for THREE or more things
'Divide the prize among the five winners.'
Exception: 'between' is OK for more than two when items are distinct and individually related.

'Being' is almost ALWAYS wrong on GMAT sentence correction.
It creates awkward passive/progressive constructions.
Wrong: 'The problem being ignored by management…'
Right: 'The problem ignored by management…'
When you see 'being,' it's usually a wrong answer choice.

The subject of a sentence must logically match its predicate.
Wrong: 'The reason for the delay is because of traffic.'
('reason… is because' is redundant — 'because' already implies reason)
Right: 'The delay was caused by traffic.' or 'The reason was traffic.'

Correct GMAT idioms for comparisons:
• 'more X than Y' (not 'more X as Y')
• 'as X as Y' (equality)
• 'the same as' (not 'the same like' or 'the same than')
• 'X times as many as Y' (not 'X times more than')
Always check that both sides of a comparison are grammatically parallel.
